title: Closed Water System Definition
summary: A closed water system has no more than one point of interface with a compressible gas or surface and will not create system flow by changes in elevation.
evidence_quote: A closed water system is defined as one with no more than one point of interface with a compressible gas or surface, and that will not create system flow by changes in elevation.

title: Open vs. Closed System Hydraulics
summary: In a closed system, flow cannot be motivated by static head differences, pumps do not provide static lift, and the entire piping system is always filled with water.
evidence_quote: in a closed system (1) flow cannot be motivated by static head differences, (2) pumps do not provide static lift, and (3) the entire piping system is always filled with water.

title: Fundamental Hydronic System Components
summary: The five fundamental components of a hydronic system are loads, source, expansion chamber, pump, and distribution system.
evidence_quote: These fundamental components are ‑ Loads ‑ Source‑ Expansion chamber‑ Pump‑ Distribution system

title: Expansion Chamber Dual Function
summary: The expansion chamber is the only component that serves both a thermal and a hydraulic function in a hydronic system.
evidence_quote: The expansion chamber is the only component that serves both a thermal and a hydraulic function.

title: System Design Step: Determine Loads
summary: First step in hydronic system design is to determine system and zone loads, which determine flow and affect heat transfer ability and energy performance.
evidence_quote: 1.Determine system and zone loads. … The load, in conjunction with design water temperature difference, determines the flow of the hydronic system, which ultimately affects the system’s heat transfer ability and energy performance.

title: System Design Step: Select Heat Transfer Devices
summary: Selecting comfort or process heat transfer devices (e.g., coils) has the single largest influence on hydronic system design.
evidence_quote: 2.Select comfort or process heat transfer devices. … Coil selection and operation has the single largest influence on hydronic system design.

title: System Design Step: Select Air Management
summary: Selecting an air management methodology is essential as all hydronic systems entrain air, and managing its collection is key to system pressure and safe operation.
evidence_quote: 8.Select air management methodology. … Managing the collection of that air as it leaves the working fluid is essential to management of system pressure and the safe operation of system components.

title: System Design Step: Select Pump
summary: Proper pump selection is critical to limit energy use in transporting fluid through the distribution system; improper selection leads to inefficiency and poor performance.
evidence_quote: 9.Select pump (hydraulic components). … Proper pump selection limits this energy use, whereas improper selection leads to energy inefficiency and poor distribution and heat transfer.

title: Ceiling Plenum Return Air Temperature
summary: The air temperature in a return air plenum directly below a roof deck is usually 3 to 5°F higher during the air-conditioning season than in a ducted return.
evidence_quote: The air temperature in a return air plenum directly below a roof deck is usually higher by 3 to 5°F during the air-conditioning season than in a ducted return.

title: Underfloor Air Distribution (UFAD) Application
summary: Using a raised floor for air distribution (UFAD) is popular for computer rooms and cleanrooms and can reduce overall first cost and improve air circulation to the occupied area.
evidence_quote: Using a raised floor for air distribution is popular for computer rooms and cleanrooms, and is now being used in other HVAC applications. … This UFAD system improves air circulation to the occupied area of the space.

title: Pipe vs. Ductwork Space Requirement
summary: Pipe distribution requires less ceiling space to install when compared to air ductwork, which can influence floor-to-floor height requirements.
evidence_quote: it requires less ceiling space to install pipe when compared to air ductwork.

title: Enhanced Filtration for Security
summary: Enhanced particulate filtration (MERV 14 to 16) for air-handling systems reduces circulation of dangerous substances and provides better indoor air quality.
evidence_quote: Enhanced particulate filtration for air-handling systems to the level of MERV 14 to 16 filters not only tends to reduce circulation of dangerous substances (e.g., anthrax), but also provides better indoor air quality (IAQ).

title: Outdoor Air Intake Location for Security
summary: Consideration should be given to protecting outdoor air intakes against insertion of dangerous materials by locating the intakes on the roof or substantially above grade level.
evidence_quote: consideration should be given to protecting outdoor air intakes against insertion of dangerous materials by locating the intakes on the roof or substantially above grade level.

title: Separate Systems for Vulnerable Spaces
summary: Separate systems for mailrooms, loading docks, and similar spaces should be considered to prevent spread of dangerous material received there throughout the building.
evidence_quote: Separate systems for mailrooms, loading docks, and other similar spaces should be considered so that any dangerous material received cannot be spread throughout the building from these types of vulnerable spaces.

title: Risk Assessment for Building Security
summary: The first consideration for protecting buildings against terrorist attacks via HVAC systems is to perform a risk assessment based on usage, size, population, and/or significance.
evidence_quote: The first consideration should be to perform a risk assessment of the particular facility, which may be based on usage, size, population, and/or significance.
